in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), [Oriental Orthodox Church](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Church "Oriental Orthodox Church"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") and [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) In Catholic traditions, Joseph is regarded as the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of workers and is associated with various [feast days](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day"). The month of March is dedicated to Saint Joseph. [Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX") declared him to be both the patron and the protector of the Catholic Church, in addition to his patronages of the sick and of a holy death, due to the belief that he died in the presence of Jesus and Mary. Joseph has become patron of various [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") and places. Being a patron saint of virgins, he is venerated as "most chaste".[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The veneration of the pure and most [Chaste Heart of Joseph](/wiki/Chaste_Heart_of_Joseph "Chaste Heart of Joseph") has, in contrast to the [Most Sacred Heart of Jesus](/wiki/Sacred_Heart "Sacred Heart") and the [Immaculate Heart of Mary](/wiki/Immaculate_Heart_of_Mary "Immaculate Heart of Mary"), no liturgical *cultus*, but is a private devotion.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Husband of Mary and legal father of Jesus

This article is about the husband of Mary, the mother of Jesus. For other uses, see [Saint Joseph (disambiguation)](/wiki/Saint_Joseph_(disambiguation) "Saint Joseph (disambiguation)") and [St. Joseph the Worker (disambiguation)](/wiki/St._Joseph_the_Worker_(disambiguation) "St. Joseph the Worker (disambiguation)").

According to the [canonical Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel"), **Joseph**[[2]](#cite_note-2) was a 1st-century [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") man of [Nazareth](/wiki/Nazareth "Nazareth") who was [married](/wiki/Espousals_of_the_Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Espousals of the Blessed Virgin Mary") to [Mary, the mother of Jesus](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus"), and was the legal father of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Joseph is venerated as **Saint Joseph** in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), [Oriental Orthodox Church](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Church "Oriental Orthodox Church"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") and [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) In Catholic traditions, Joseph is regarded as the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of workers and is associated with various [feast days](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day"). The month of March is dedicated to Saint Joseph. [Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX") declared him to be both the patron and the protector of the Catholic Church, in addition to his patronages of the sick and of a holy death, due to the belief that he died in the presence of Jesus and Mary. Joseph has become patron of various [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") and places. Being a patron saint of virgins, he is venerated as "most chaste".[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The veneration of the pure and most [Chaste Heart of Joseph](/wiki/Chaste_Heart_of_Joseph "Chaste Heart of Joseph") has, in contrast to the [Most Sacred Heart of Jesus](/wiki/Sacred_Heart "Sacred Heart") and the [Immaculate Heart of Mary](/wiki/Immaculate_Heart_of_Mary "Immaculate Heart of Mary"), no liturgical *cultus*, but is a private devotion.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Several venerated images of Saint Joseph have been granted a decree of [canonical coronation](/wiki/List_of_canonically_crowned_images#Pontifically_crowned_Josephian_images "List of canonically crowned images") by a [pontiff](/wiki/Pontiff "Pontiff"). Religious [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography") often depicts him with [lilies](/wiki/Lilium "Lilium") or [spikenard](/wiki/Spikenard "Spikenard"). With the present-day growth of [Mariology](/wiki/Mariology "Mariology"), the theological field of [Josephology](/wiki/Josephology "Josephology") has also grown and since the 1950s centers for studying it have been formed.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## In the New Testament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: In the New Testament")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Gerard_Seghers_-_Dream_of_St._Joseph.jpg/330px-Gerard_Seghers_-_Dream_of_St._Joseph.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gerard_Seghers_-_Dream_of_St._Joseph.jpg)

*Dream of St Joseph*, c. 1625–1630, by [Gerard Seghers](/wiki/Gerard_Seghers "Gerard Seghers")

The first appearance of Joseph is in the gospels of [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke"), often dated from around 80-90 AD. Each contains a [genealogy of Jesus](/wiki/Genealogy_of_Jesus "Genealogy of Jesus") showing ancestry from King [David](/wiki/David "David"), but through different sons; Matthew follows the major royal line from [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon"), while Luke traces another line back to [Nathan](/wiki/Nathan_(son_of_David) "Nathan (son of David)"), another son of David and [Bathsheba](/wiki/Bathsheba "Bathsheba"). Consequently, all the names between David and Joseph are different.

Like the two differing genealogies, the infancy narratives appear only in Matthew and Luke and take different approaches to reconciling the requirement that the [Messiah](/wiki/Messiah "Messiah") be born in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem") with the tradition that Jesus in fact came from [Nazareth](/wiki/Nazareth "Nazareth"). In Matthew, Joseph obeys the direction of an angel to marry Mary. Following the birth of Jesus in Bethlehem, Joseph is told by an angel in a dream to [take the family to Egypt](/wiki/Flight_into_Egypt "Flight into Egypt") to escape the [massacre of the children of Bethlehem](/wiki/Massacre_of_the_Innocents "Massacre of the Innocents") planned by Herod, the ruler of the [Roman province of Judea](/wiki/Judaea_(Roman_province) "Judaea (Roman province)"). Once Herod has died, an angel tells Joseph to return but to avoid Herod's son, and he takes his wife and the child to Nazareth in [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee") and settles there. Thus in Matthew, the infant Jesus, like Moses, is in peril from a cruel king, like Moses he has a (fore)father named [Joseph](/wiki/Joseph_(Genesis) "Joseph (Genesis)") who goes down to Egypt, like the Old Testament Joseph this Joseph has a father named Jacob, and both Josephs receive important dreams foretelling their future.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In the Gospel of Luke, Joseph already lives in Nazareth, and Jesus is born in Bethlehem because Joseph and Mary have to travel there to be counted in a census. Subsequently, Jesus was born there. Luke's account makes no mention of him being visited by angels (Mary and various others instead receive similar visitations), the Massacre of the Innocents, or of the flight into Egypt.

The last time Joseph appears in person in any of the canonical Gospels is in the narrative of the [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") [visit to the Temple](/wiki/Finding_in_the_Temple "Finding in the Temple") in Jerusalem when Jesus is 12 years old, which is found only in Luke. No mention is made of him thereafter.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The story emphasizes Jesus' awareness of his coming mission: here Jesus speaks to both Mary and Joseph of "my father," meaning God, but they fail to understand.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Joseph is not mentioned as being present at the [Wedding at Cana](/wiki/Wedding_at_Cana "Wedding at Cana") at the beginning of Jesus' mission, nor at the [Passion](/wiki/Passion_of_Jesus "Passion of Jesus") at the end. If he had been present at the [Crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), he would under Jewish custom have been expected to take charge of Jesus' body, but this role is instead performed by [Joseph of Arimathea](/wiki/Joseph_of_Arimathea "Joseph of Arimathea"). Nor would Jesus have entrusted his mother to the care of [John the Apostle](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle") if her husband had been alive.[[15]](#cite_note-CatholicEncyc-15)

While none of the Gospels mentions Joseph as present at any event during Jesus' adult ministry, the [synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels") share a scene in which the people of Nazareth, Jesus' hometown, doubt Jesus' status as a prophet because they know his family. In [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark") 6:3, they call Jesus "Mary's son" instead of naming his father. In [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), the townspeople call Jesus "the [carpenter's](/wiki/Carpentry "Carpentry") son," again without naming his father.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") 3:23 NIV: "Now Jesus himself was about thirty years old when he began his ministry. He was the son, so it was thought, of Joseph, the son of Heli,"[[17]](#cite_note-17). In Luke the tone of the contemporary people is positive, whereas in Mark and Matthew it is disparaging.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermès20041–37-18) This incident does not appear in [John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John"), but in a parallel story, the disbelieving neighbors refer to "Jesus the son of Joseph, whose father and mother we know".[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The [Pauline epistles](/wiki/Pauline_epistles "Pauline epistles") are the oldest extant Christian writings.[[20]](#cite_note-20) These mention Jesus' mother (without naming her), but do not refer to his father. The [Gospel of Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark"), believed to be the first gospel to be written and with a date about two decades after [Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle"), also does not mention Jesus' father.[[21]](#cite_note-oblates-21)

### Lineage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lineage")]

Further information: [Genealogy of Jesus](/wiki/Genealogy_of_Jesus "Genealogy of Jesus")

Joseph appears in Luke as the father of Jesus and in a "variant reading in Matthew".[[39]](#cite_note-Vermes20-39) [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") both contain a [genealogy of Jesus](/wiki/Genealogy_of_Jesus "Genealogy of Jesus") showing his ancestry from [David](/wiki/David "David"), but through different sons; Matthew follows the major royal line from [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon"), while Luke traces another line back to [Nathan](/wiki/Nathan_(son_of_David) "Nathan (son of David)"), another son of David and [Bathsheba](/wiki/Bathsheba "Bathsheba"). Consequently, all the names between David and Joseph are different. According to Matthew 1:16 "Jacob begat Joseph the husband of Mary",[[40]](#cite_note-40) while according to Luke 3:23, Joseph is said to be "the son of [Heli](/wiki/Heli_(Bible) "Heli (Bible)")".[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The variances between the genealogies given in Matthew and Luke are explained in a number of ways. One possibility is that Matthew's genealogy traces Jesus' legal descent, according to [Jewish law](/wiki/Halakha "Halakha"), through Joseph; while Luke's genealogy traces his actual physical descent through Mary.[[42]](#cite_note-Ironside-42)[[43]](#cite_note-Ryrie-43) Another possibility proposed by [Julius Africanus](/wiki/Sextus_Julius_Africanus "Sextus Julius Africanus") is that both Joseph and his father were the sons of Levirate marriages.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) A third explanation proposed by [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") is that Joseph was adopted, and his two genealogies trace Joseph's lineage through his biological and adopted families.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Professional life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Professional life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/John_Everett_Millais_-_Christ_in_the_House_of_His_Parents_%28%60The_Carpenter%27s_Shop%27%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/330px-John_Everett_Millais_-_Christ_in_the_House_of_His_Parents_%28%60The_Carpenter%27s_Shop%27%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Everett_Millais_-_Christ_in_the_House_of_His_Parents_(%60The_Carpenter%27s_Shop%27)_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*[Christ in the House of his Parents](/wiki/Christ_in_the_House_of_his_Parents "Christ in the House of his Parents")*, 1850, by [John Everett Millais](/wiki/John_Everett_Millais "John Everett Millais")

In the Gospels, Joseph's occupation is mentioned only once. The [Gospel of Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") asks about Jesus:

> Is not this the carpenter's son (*ho tou tektōnos huios*)?[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Joseph's description as a "*[tekton](/wiki/Tekton "Tekton")*" (τέκτων) has been traditionally translated into English as "carpenter", but is a rather general word (from the same root that gives us "technical", "technology")[[48]](#cite_note-48) that could cover makers of objects in various materials.[[49]](#cite_note-49) The Greek term evokes an artisan with wood in general, or an artisan in iron or stone.[[50]](#cite_note-Deiss14-50) But the specific association with woodworking is a constant in [Early Christian](/wiki/Early_Christian "Early Christian") tradition; [Justin Martyr](/wiki/Justin_Martyr "Justin Martyr") (died c. 165) wrote that Jesus made [yokes](/wiki/Yoke "Yoke") and [ploughs](/wiki/Plough "Plough"), and there are similar early references.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Georges_de_La_Tour._St._Joseph%2C_the_Carpenter.JPG/250px-Georges_de_La_Tour._St._Joseph%2C_the_Carpenter.JPG)](/wiki/File:Georges_de_La_Tour._St._Joseph,_the_Carpenter.JPG)

*[Joseph the Carpenter](/wiki/Joseph_the_Carpenter "Joseph the Carpenter")*, by [Georges de La Tour](/wiki/Georges_de_La_Tour "Georges de La Tour"), c. 1645

Other scholars have argued that *tekton* could equally mean a highly skilled craftsman in wood or the more prestigious metal, perhaps running a workshop with several employees, and noted sources recording the shortage of skilled artisans at the time.[[52]](#cite_note-52) [Géza Vermes](/wiki/G%C3%A9za_Vermes "Géza Vermes") has stated that the terms 'carpenter' and 'son of a carpenter' are used in the Jewish [Talmud](/wiki/Talmud "Talmud") to signify a very learned man, and he suggests that a description of Joseph as 'naggar' (a carpenter) could indicate that he was considered wise and highly literate in the Torah.[[53]](#cite_note-53)
At the time of Joseph, [Nazareth](/wiki/Nazareth "Nazareth") was an obscure village in [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee"), about 130 kilometres (81 mi) from the Holy City of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), and is barely mentioned in surviving non-Christian texts and documents.[[54]](#cite_note-MisJ-54)[[55]](#cite_note-EJ1-55)[[56]](#cite_note-TM1998-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) [Archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") over most of the site is made impossible by subsequent building, but from what has been excavated and tombs in the area around the village, it is estimated that the population was at most about 400.[[58]](#cite_note-58) It was, however, only about 6 kilometers from the city of [Sepphoris](/wiki/Sepphoris "Sepphoris"), which was destroyed and depopulated by the Romans in 4 BC, and thereafter was extensively rebuilt. Analysis of the landscape and other evidence suggest that in Joseph's lifetime Nazareth was "oriented toward" the nearby city,[[59]](#cite_note-59) which had an overwhelmingly Jewish population although with many signs of [Hellenization](/wiki/Hellenization "Hellenization"),[[60]](#cite_note-60) and historians have speculated that Joseph and later Jesus too might have traveled daily to work on the rebuilding. Specifically the large theatre in the city has been suggested, although this has aroused much controversy over dating and other issues.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Other scholars see Joseph and Jesus as the general village craftsmen, working in wood, stone, and metal on a wide variety of jobs.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Modern appraisal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern appraisal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Jacques_Stella_-_Mort_de_Saint_Joseph.jpg/250px-Jacques_Stella_-_Mort_de_Saint_Joseph.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacques_Stella_-_Mort_de_Saint_Joseph.jpg)

*Death of Saint Joseph*, following the apocryphal account. [Jacques Stella](/wiki/Jacques_Stella "Jacques Stella"), 1650s

The name "Joseph" is found almost exclusively in the genealogies and the infancy narratives.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermès2004398–417-63)[[64]](#cite_note-ActJBirth-64)

The canonical gospels created a problem: they stated clearly that Mary conceives Jesus virginally, and Joseph is not his father; however, Jesus was described unambiguously by John and Matthew as "Joseph's son" and "the carpenter's son", and Joseph's paternity was essential to establish Jesus' [Davidic descent](/wiki/Davidic_line "Davidic line"). The theological situation was complicated by the gospel references to ["brothers and sisters" of Jesus](/wiki/Brothers_of_Jesus "Brothers of Jesus"),[[65]](#cite_note-Joseph629-65) who may have been

1. the sons of Mary, the mother of Jesus, and Joseph;
2. sons of [Mary, the wife of Clopas](/wiki/Mary,_the_wife_of_Clopas "Mary, the wife of Clopas") and sister of Mary the mother of Jesus; or
3. sons of Joseph by a former marriage.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrossLivingstone2005237-238-66)

Modern positions on the question of the relationship between Joseph and the Virgin Mary vary. The [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), which names Joseph's first wife as Salome, holds that Joseph was a widower and betrothed to Mary,[[67]](#cite_note-67) and that references to Jesus' "brothers" were children of Joseph from a previous marriage. A popular position held by many Catholics, derived from the writings of [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome"), is that Joseph was the husband of Mary, but that references to Jesus' "brothers" should be understood to mean cousins. Such usage is prevalent throughout history, and occurs elsewhere in the Bible. [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham")'s nephew Lot (Genesis 11:26-28) was referred to as his brother (Genesis 14:14), as was [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob")'s uncle Laban (Genesis 29:15). Jesus himself frequently used the word "brother" as a generic term for one's fellow man. This custom has continued into modern times, with close friends, colleagues, and fellow churchgoers often called "brothers and sisters." Generally, most Protestants read "brothers and sisters" of Jesus as referring specifically to children born of Mary.

The [perpetual virginity of Mary](/wiki/Perpetual_virginity_of_Mary "Perpetual virginity of Mary") is a Christian doctrine that Mary, the mother of Jesus, was a virgin "before, during and after" the birth of Christ, which means that Joseph and Mary never had sexual relations. The Catholic Church adheres to this doctrine, as do some Lutherans, Anglicans, Reformed, and other Protestants.

The term *[kiddushin](/wiki/Erusin "Erusin")*, which refers to the first part of a two-part marriage, is frequently translated as "betrothal". Couples who fulfill the requirements of the *kiddushin* are married, until death or divorce.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-Barclay1998-69)

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Death")]

The New Testament has no mention of Joseph's death, but he is never mentioned after the story of finding Jesus in the temple when Jesus was 12.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) Some eastern traditions made Joseph out to be 90 years old, a tradition intended to protect Mary's perpetual virginity.

[Mauro Gagliardi](/w/index.php?title=Mauro_Gagliardi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mauro Gagliardi (page does not exist)") presents a hypothesis that Joseph was [assumed into Heaven](/wiki/Entering_heaven_alive "Entering heaven alive") with body and soul because he belongs to the few saints who left no bodily relics.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

## Later apocryphal writings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Later apocryphal writings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Sagrada_Familia_del_pajarito_%28Murillo%29.jpg/250px-Sagrada_Familia_del_pajarito_%28Murillo%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sagrada_Familia_del_pajarito_(Murillo).jpg)

*[The Holy Family with a Little Bird](/wiki/The_Holy_Family_with_a_Little_Bird "The Holy Family with a Little Bird")*, c. 1650, by [Bartolomé Esteban Murillo](/wiki/Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo "Bartolomé Esteban Murillo")

The [Gospel of James](/wiki/Gospel_of_James "Gospel of James") (also known as the Protoevangelium of James), written about 150 AD, presents Joseph as an old man chosen by God to watch over the Virgin. Jesus' brothers are presented as Joseph's children by an earlier marriage.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

The *[History of Joseph the Carpenter](/wiki/History_of_Joseph_the_Carpenter "History of Joseph the Carpenter")*, written in the 5th century and framed as a biography of Joseph dictated by Jesus, describes how Joseph, aged 90, a widower with four sons (Judas, Justus, James, and Simon) and two daughters (Assia and Lydia), is given charge of the twelve-year-old Mary, who then lives in his household raising his youngest son James the Less until she is ready to be married at age 14½. Joseph's death aged 111, attended by angels and asserting the [perpetual virginity of Mary](/wiki/Perpetual_virginity_of_Mary "Perpetual virginity of Mary"), takes up approximately half the story.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

## Church Fathers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Church Fathers")]

According to the bishop of Salamis, [Epiphanius](/wiki/Epiphanius_of_Salamis "Epiphanius of Salamis"), in his work *[The Panarion](/wiki/Panarion "Panarion")* (AD 374–375) Joseph became the father of [James](/wiki/James_the_brother_of_Jesus "James the brother of Jesus") and his three brothers ([Joses](/wiki/Joses,_brother_of_James_the_younger "Joses, brother of James the younger"), [Simeon](/wiki/Simon,_brother_of_Jesus "Simon, brother of Jesus"), [Judah](/wiki/Jude,_brother_of_Jesus "Jude, brother of Jesus")) and two sisters (a Salome and a Mary[[75]](#cite_note-75) or a Salome and an Anna[[76]](#cite_note-76)) with James being the eldest sibling. James and his siblings were not children of Mary but were Joseph's children from a previous marriage. After Joseph's first wife died, many years later when he was eighty, "he took Mary (mother of Jesus)".[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

[Eusebius of Caesarea](/wiki/Eusebius_of_Caesarea "Eusebius of Caesarea") relates in his *[Church History](/wiki/Church_History_(Eusebius) "Church History (Eusebius)")* (Book III, ch. 11) that "[Hegesippus](/wiki/Hegesippus_(chronicler) "Hegesippus (chronicler)") records that [Clopas](/wiki/Clopas "Clopas") was a brother of Joseph and an uncle of Jesus."[[79]](#cite_note-79) [Epiphanius](/wiki/Epiphanius_of_Salamis "Epiphanius of Salamis") adds that Joseph and Cleopas were brothers, sons of "Jacob, surnamed Panther."[[80]](#cite_note-80)

[Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen") quotes the [Greek philosopher](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_philosophy "Ancient Greek philosophy") and opponent of [early Christianity](/wiki/History_of_Christianity "History of Christianity") [Celsus](/wiki/Celsus "Celsus") (from his work *[On the True Doctrine](/wiki/The_True_Word "The True Word")*, c. 178 AD) as controversially asserting that Joseph left Mary upon learning of her pregnancy: "...when she was pregnant she was turned out of doors by the carpenter to whom she had been betrothed, as having been guilty of adultery, and that she bore a child to a certain soldier named [Pantera](/wiki/Tiberius_Julius_Abdes_Pantera "Tiberius Julius Abdes Pantera")."[[81]](#cite_note-81) Origen, however, argues that Celsus's claim was a fabricated story.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

## Veneration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Veneration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Martin_Schongauer_%281440-45_-_1491%29_-_The_Nativity_of_Christ_with_the_Adoration_of_the_Shepherds_-_1629_-_Gem%C3%A4ldegalerie.jpg/250px-Martin_Schongauer_%281440-45_-_1491%29_-_The_Nativity_of_Christ_with_the_Adoration_of_the_Shepherds_-_1629_-_Gem%C3%A4ldegalerie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Schongauer_(1440-45_-_1491)_-_The_Nativity_of_Christ_with_the_Adoration_of_the_Shepherds_-_1629_-_Gem%C3%A4ldegalerie.jpg)

*The Nativity of Christ* by [Martin Schongauer](/wiki/Martin_Schongauer "Martin Schongauer") (1475–1480)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo_-_The_Heavenly_and_Earthly_Trinities_-_1681-82.jpg/250px-Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo_-_The_Heavenly_and_Earthly_Trinities_-_1681-82.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo_-_The_Heavenly_and_Earthly_Trinities_-_1681-82.jpg)

*Holy Family with the Holy Trinity* by [Murillo](/wiki/Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo "Bartolomé Esteban Murillo"), 1675–1682

The earliest records of a formal devotional following for Saint Joseph date to the year 800 and references to him as *Nutritor Domini* (educator/guardian of the Lord) began to appear in the 9th century, and continued growing to the 14th century.[[83]](#cite_note-Irénée_Henri_Dalmais_page_143-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas") discussed the necessity of the presence of Saint Joseph in the plan of the Incarnation for if Mary had not been married, the Jews would have stoned her and that in his youth Jesus needed the care and protection of a human father.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

In the 15th century, major steps were taken by [Bernardine of Siena](/wiki/Bernardine_of_Siena "Bernardine of Siena"), Pierre d'Ailly, and [Jean Gerson](/wiki/Jean_Gerson "Jean Gerson").[[83]](#cite_note-Irénée_Henri_Dalmais_page_143-83) Gerson wrote *Consideration sur Saint Joseph* and preached sermons on Saint Joseph at the [Council of Constance](/wiki/Council_of_Constance "Council of Constance").[[88]](#cite_note-88) In 1889 [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII") issued the encyclical *[Quamquam pluries](/wiki/Quamquam_pluries "Quamquam pluries")* in which he urged Catholics to pray to Saint Joseph, as the patron of the church in view of the challenges facing the church. Likewise, Leo stated that Saint Joseph "set himself to protect with a mighty love and a daily solicitude his spouse and the Divine Infant; regularly by his work he earned what was necessary for the one and the other for nourishment and clothing"[[89]](#cite_note-89)

[Josephology](/wiki/Josephology "Josephology"), the [theological](/wiki/Theology "Theology") study of Saint Joseph, is one of the most recent theological disciplines.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In 1989, on the occasion of the centenary of *Quamquam pluries* Pope [John Paul II](/wiki/John_Paul_II "John Paul II") issued *[Redemptoris Custos](/wiki/Redemptoris_Custos "Redemptoris Custos")* (*Guardian of the Redeemer*), which presented Saint Joseph's role in the plan of redemption, as part of the "redemption documents" issued by John Paul II such as *[Redemptoris Mater](/wiki/Redemptoris_Mater "Redemptoris Mater")* to which it refers.[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)

Together with the [Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Blessed Virgin Mary") and the [Child Jesus](/wiki/Child_Jesus "Child Jesus"), Joseph is one of the three members of the [Holy Family](/wiki/Holy_Family "Holy Family"); since he only appears in the birth narratives of the Gospels, Jesus is depicted as a child when with him. The formal veneration of the Holy Family began in the 17th century by [François de Laval](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Laval "François de Laval").

In 1962, [Pope John XXIII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXIII "Pope John XXIII") inserted the name of Joseph in the [Canon of the Mass](/wiki/Canon_of_the_Mass "Canon of the Mass"), immediately after that of the Blessed Virgin Mary. In 2013, [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") had his name added to the three other [Eucharistic Prayers](/wiki/Eucharistic_Prayer "Eucharistic Prayer").[[95]](#cite_note-95)

#### Saint Joseph's Day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Saint Joseph's Day")]

Main article: [Saint Joseph's Day](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Day "Saint Joseph's Day")

19 March, Saint Joseph's Day, has been the principal [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") of Saint Joseph in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity")[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97) since the 10th century, and is celebrated by Catholics, [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), and other denominations.[[98]](#cite_note-98) In [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"), the feast day of Saint Joseph is celebrated on 26 December (Synaxis of the Mother of God and [flight of the Holy Family into Egypt](/wiki/Flight_into_Egypt "Flight into Egypt")),[[99]](#cite_note-99) the First Sunday after the [Nativity of Christ](/wiki/Nativity_of_Christ "Nativity of Christ"), on 19 March and on the Sunday of the Holy Forefathers (two Sundays before the [Nativity](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas")) and on the Sunday of the Holy Fathers (Sunday before the [Nativity](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas")), when he is commemorated together with other [ancestors of Jesus](/wiki/Genealogy_of_Jesus "Genealogy of Jesus").[[100]](#cite_note-100) In the Catholic Church, the Feast of Saint Joseph (19 March) is a [solemnity](/wiki/Solemnity "Solemnity") (first class if using the [Tridentine calendar](/wiki/Tridentine_calendar "Tridentine calendar")), and is transferred to another date if impeded (i.e., 19 March falling on Sunday or in Holy Week).[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Joseph is [remembered](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints_(Church_of_England) "Calendar of saints (Church of England)") in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints_(Episcopal_Church) "Calendar of saints (Episcopal Church)") on 19 March.[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103)

Popular customs among Christians of various liturgical traditions observing Saint Joseph's Day are attending [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") or the [Divine Service](/wiki/Divine_Service_(Lutheran) "Divine Service (Lutheran)"), wearing red-coloured clothing, carrying dried [fava beans](/wiki/Fava_beans "Fava beans") that have been [blessed](/wiki/Blessing#Christianity "Blessing"), and assembling [home altars](/wiki/Home_altar "Home altar") dedicated to Saint Joseph.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

In [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), where Saint Joseph is regarded by many as their [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint"), and in many [Italian-American](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans") communities, thanks are given to Saint Joseph (*San Giuseppe* in [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language")) for preventing a famine in Sicily during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). According to legend, there was a severe drought at the time, and the people prayed for their patron saint to bring them rain. They promised that if God answered their prayers through Joseph's intercession, they would prepare a large feast to honor him. The rain did come, and the people of Sicily prepared a large banquet for their patron saint. The [fava bean](/wiki/Fava_bean "Fava bean") was the crop which saved the population from starvation and is a traditional part of Saint Joseph's Day altars and traditions. Giving food to the needy is a Saint Joseph's Day custom. In some communities it is traditional to wear red clothing and eat a Neapolitan pastry known as a [zeppola](/wiki/Zeppola "Zeppola") (created in 1840 by Don Pasquale Pinatauro in Naples) on Saint Joseph's Day.[[105]](#cite_note-non-stop-105) *Maccu di San Giuseppe* is a traditional Sicilian dish that consists of various ingredients and [maccu](/wiki/Maccu "Maccu") that is prepared on this day.[[106]](#cite_note-Clarkson-106) Maccu is a foodstuff and soup that dates to [ancient](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history") times which is prepared with fava beans as a primary ingredient.[[106]](#cite_note-Clarkson-106)

Upon a typical Saint Joseph's Day altar, people place flowers, limes, candles, wine, fava beans, specially prepared cakes, breads, cookies, other meatless dishes, and [zeppole](/wiki/Zeppole "Zeppole"). Foods are traditionally served containing bread crumbs to represent sawdust since Joseph was a carpenter. Because the feast occurs during Lent, traditionally no meat was allowed on the celebration table. The altar usually has three tiers, to represent the Trinity.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

#### Saint Joseph the Worker

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Saint Joseph the Worker")]

In 1870, [Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX") declared Joseph patron of the [Universal Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and instituted another feast, a solemnity to be held on the third Sunday of Eastertide. Pope Pius X, in order to restore the celebration of Sundays, moved this feast to the Wednesday in the second week after Easter, and gave it an [octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgy) "Octave (liturgy)"). In 1955, Pope [Pius XII](/wiki/Pius_XII "Pius XII") introduced in its place the feast of *Saint Joseph the Worker* on 1 May in the [General Roman Calendar](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar_of_Pope_Pius_XII "General Roman Calendar of Pope Pius XII") as an ecclesiastical counterpart to the [International Workers' Day](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Day "International Workers' Day") on the same day.[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109) This reflects Saint Joseph's status as patron of workers. Pius XII established the feast both to honor Saint Joseph, and to make people aware of the dignity of human work.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

#### Espousals of the Blessed Virgin Mary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Espousals of the Blessed Virgin Mary")]

The [Espousals of the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Espousals_of_the_Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Espousals of the Blessed Virgin Mary") is observed in some liturgical calendars (e. g. that of the [Oblates of Saint Joseph](/wiki/Oblates_of_Saint_Joseph "Oblates of Saint Joseph")) on 23 January.

### *Patris corde* and *Year of Saint Joseph*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Patris corde and Year of Saint Joseph")]

Pope Francis on 8 December 2020, released the [apostolic letter](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_letter#Letters_of_the_popes_in_modern_times "Ecclesiastical letter") *Patris corde* on the occasion of the 150th anniversary of the declaration by Pius IX, on 8 December 1870, of Saint Joseph as patron of the Universal Church; for the same reason he declared a *Year of Saint Joseph*, from 8 December 2020, to 8 December 2021.[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)

## Patronage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Patronage")]

[Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX") proclaimed Saint Joseph the patron of the Universal Church in 1870. Having died in the "arms of Jesus and Mary" according to Catholic tradition, he is considered the model of the pious believer who receives grace at the moment of death, in other words, the patron of a happy death.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

Saint Joseph is well known as the patron saint of fathers, both families and virgins, workers, especially carpenters, expecting mothers and unborn children. Among many others, he is the patron saint of attorneys and barristers, emigrants, travelers and house hunters. He is invoked against hesitation and for the grace of a holy death.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

[Saint Joseph's Oratory](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Oratory "Saint Joseph's Oratory"), [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), the largest church in Canada

Many cities, towns, and locations are named after Saint Joseph. According to the [National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency](/wiki/National_Geospatial-Intelligence_Agency "National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency"), the [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") form, *San Jose*, is the most common place name in the world. Probably the most-recognized San Joses are [San José, Costa Rica](/wiki/San_Jos%C3%A9,_Costa_Rica "San José, Costa Rica"), and [San Jose, California](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California"), United States, given their name by [Spanish colonists](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas"). Joseph is the patron saint of the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World")[[115]](#cite_note-115) and of many countries ([Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") – especially venerated in [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(state) "Carinthia (state)"), [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"), and [Tyrol](/wiki/State_of_Tyrol "State of Tyrol"),[[116]](#cite_note-116) [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") – proclaimed as a patron saint of [Kingdom of Croatia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(Habsburg) "Kingdom of Croatia (Habsburg)") by [Croatian Sabor](/wiki/Croatian_Sabor "Croatian Sabor") on 9 June 1687[[117]](#cite_note-117)) and of several main cities ([Karlovac](/wiki/Karlovac "Karlovac")[[118]](#cite_note-DanKa-118)), dioceses and administrative regions ([Karlovac County](/wiki/Karlovac_County "Karlovac County") in Croatia[[118]](#cite_note-DanKa-118)).

Many churches, monasteries and other institutions are dedicated to Saint Joseph. [Saint Joseph's Oratory](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Oratory "Saint Joseph's Oratory") is the largest church in Canada, with the largest dome of its kind in the world after that of [Saint Peter's Basilica](/wiki/Saint_Peter%27s_Basilica "Saint Peter's Basilica") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). Elsewhere in the world churches named after the saint may be known as those of *San Giuseppe*, e.g. [San Giuseppe dei Teatini](/wiki/San_Giuseppe_dei_Teatini "San Giuseppe dei Teatini"), *San José*, e.g. [Metropolitan Cathedral of San José](/wiki/Metropolitan_Cathedral_of_San_Jos%C3%A9 "Metropolitan Cathedral of San José") or *São José*, e.g. in [Porto Alegre](/wiki/Porto_Alegre "Porto Alegre"), Brazil.

The [Sisters of St. Joseph](/wiki/Sisters_of_St._Joseph "Sisters of St. Joseph") were founded as an order in 1650 and have about 14,013 members worldwide. In 1871, the [Josephite Fathers](/wiki/Josephite_Fathers "Josephite Fathers") of the Catholic Church were created under the patronage of Joseph, intending to work with the poor. The first Josephites in America re-devoted their part of the order to ministry within the newly emancipated African American community. The [Oblates of St. Joseph](/wiki/Oblates_of_St._Joseph "Oblates of St. Joseph") were founded in 1878 by [Joseph Marello](/wiki/Joseph_Marello "Joseph Marello"). In 1999 their Shrine of Saint Joseph the Guardian of the Redeemer was named after the Apostolic exhortation *[Redemptoris Custos](/wiki/Redemptoris_Custos "Redemptoris Custos")*.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

### Prayers and devotions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Prayers and devotions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Billafingen_Pfarrkirche_Seitenaltar.jpg/250px-Billafingen_Pfarrkirche_Seitenaltar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Billafingen_Pfarrkirche_Seitenaltar.jpg)

Altar of Saint Joseph, [Billafingen](/wiki/Billafingen "Billafingen"), Germany

In the Eastern Orthodox Church, during the [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") of Saint Joseph the following hymn is chanted:

> Verily, Joseph the betrothed, saw clearly in his old age that the foresayings of the Prophets had  
> been fulfilled openly; for he was given an odd earnest,  
> receiving inspiration from the angels,  
> who cried, Glory to God; for he hath bestowed peace on earth.

In the Catholic tradition, just as there are prayers for the [Seven Joys of Mary](/wiki/Seven_Joys_of_Mary "Seven Joys of Mary") and [Seven Sorrows of Mary](/wiki/Seven_Sorrows_of_Mary "Seven Sorrows of Mary"), there are also prayers for the seven joys and seven sorrows of Saint Joseph. Furthermore, there is a [novena](/wiki/Novena "Novena")[[120]](#cite_note-120) prayed before the feast of Saint Joseph on 19 March. Saint Joseph is frequently invoked for employment, daily protection, vocation, happy marriage, and a happy death.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123)

Multiple venerated Catholics have described their devotion to Saint Joseph and his intercession. [Francis de Sales](/wiki/Francis_de_Sales "Francis de Sales") included Saint Joseph along with [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") as saints to be invoked during prayers in his 1609 book, *[Introduction to the Devout Life](/wiki/Introduction_to_the_Devout_Life "Introduction to the Devout Life")*.[[124]](#cite_note-124) [Teresa of Ávila](/wiki/Teresa_of_%C3%81vila "Teresa of Ávila") attributed her recovery of health to Saint Joseph and recommended him as an advocate.[[125]](#cite_note-125) [Therese of Lisieux](/wiki/Therese_of_Lisieux "Therese of Lisieux") stated that she prayed daily to "Saint Joseph, Father and Protector of Virgins" and felt protected from danger as a result.[[126]](#cite_note-126) [Pope Pius X](/wiki/Pope_Pius_X "Pope Pius X") composed a prayer to Saint Joseph which begins:[[127]](#cite_note-127)

> Glorious St. Joseph, pattern of all who are devoted to toil,  
> obtain for me the grace to toil, in the spirit of penance,  
> in order to thereby atone for my many sins …

There is a Catholic tradition that burying a statuette of Saint Joseph on the grounds of a home will help to sell or buy[[128]](#cite_note-128) a house.;[[129]](#cite_note-129) this tradition became so popular through the World Wide Web that some American realtors bought them by the gross.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

St. Joseph's role in the Catholic church is summarized by the German theologian Friedrich Justus Knecht:

> *St. Joseph's high place in the kingdom of God* comes from this, that God chose him to be the guardian and protector of His Son, entrusting him with what was greatest and dearest to Himself, singling him out and especially blessing him for this office. The Church celebrates a Feast in honour of St. Joseph on 19 March, and desires that all the faithful should honour him, ask for his intercession, and imitate his virtues. St. Joseph is the especial patron of the Church. Even as he was the protector of the Child Jesus on earth, so, we believe, is he now the protector of the mystical Body of Jesus, His holy Church. We also especially seek his intercession for a good death, because, having died so blessedly, in the presence and with the assistance of Jesus and Mary, he should be supplicated to obtain for us from Jesus the grace of a happy death.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

## In art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Joseph&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: In art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Brooklyn_Museum_-_Saint_Joseph_with_the_Flowering_Rod_-_Jusepe_de_Ribera_-_overall.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Museum_-_Saint_Joseph_with_the_Flowering_Rod_-_Jusepe_de_Ribera_-_overall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Museum_-_Saint_Joseph_with_the_Flowering_Rod_-_Jusepe_de_Ribera_-_overall.jpg)

*Saint Joseph with the Flowering Rod*, by [Jusepe de Ribera](/wiki/Jusepe_de_Ribera "Jusepe de Ribera"), early 1630s. Ribera conveys the unexpected wonder of the moment with the lighting from above. [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum")

In mosaics in the basilica of [Santa Maria Maggiore](/wiki/Santa_Maria_Maggiore "Santa Maria Maggiore") (432-40) Joseph is portrayed young, bearded and dressed as a Roman of status.[[132]](#cite_note-132) Joseph is shown mostly with a beard, not only in keeping with Jewish custom, but also because – although the Gospel accounts do not give his age – later legends tend to present him as an old man at the time of his wedding to Mary. Earlier writers thought the traditional imagery necessary to support belief in Mary's perpetual virginity.[[133]](#cite_note-133) [Jean Gerson](/wiki/Jean_Gerson "Jean Gerson") nonetheless favoured showing him as a younger man.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/San_Jos%C3%A9_-_Alonso_Miguel_de_Tovar.jpg/250px-San_Jos%C3%A9_-_Alonso_Miguel_de_Tovar.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Jos%C3%A9_-_Alonso_Miguel_de_Tovar.jpg)

*Joseph with the Child and the Flowering Rod*, [Alonso Miguel de Tovar](/wiki/Alonso_Miguel_de_Tovar "Alonso Miguel de Tovar")

In recent centuries – in step with a growing interest in Joseph's role in Gospel exegesis – he himself has become a focal figure in representations of the Holy Family. He is now often portrayed as a younger or even youthful man (perhaps especially in [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") depictions), whether going about his work as a carpenter, or participating actively in the daily life of Mary and Jesus as an equal and openly affectionate member.[[135]](#cite_note-135) Art critic and self proclaimed atheist [Waldemar Januszczak](/wiki/Waldemar_Januszczak "Waldemar Januszczak") however emphasises the preponderance of Joseph's representation as an old man, and sees this as the need.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

However Carolyn Wilson challenges the long-held view that pre-Tridentine images were often intended to demean him.[[137]](#cite_note-137) According to Charlene Villaseñor Black, "Seventeenth-century Spanish and Mexican artists reconceptualized Joseph as an important figure, ... representing him as the youthful, physically robust, diligent head of the Holy Family."[[138]](#cite_note-138) In [Bartolomé Esteban Murillo](/wiki/Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo "Bartolomé Esteban Murillo")'s *[The Heavenly and Earthly Trinities](/wiki/The_Heavenly_and_Earthly_Trinities "The Heavenly and Earthly Trinities")*, Saint Joseph is given the same prominence as the Virgin as they are both part of the "Earthly Trinity" and Jesus lays his hands on both of them.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/St._Joseph_on_St._Patrick%27s_Cathedral_in_New_York_City.jpg/250px-St._Joseph_on_St._Patrick%27s_Cathedral_in_New_York_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Joseph_on_St._Patrick%27s_Cathedral_in_New_York_City.jpg)

Sculpture of Saint Joseph on the door of [St. Patrick's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Cathedral_(New_York_City) "St. Patrick's Cathedral (New York City)") in New York City.

Full cycles of his life are rare in the Middle Ages, although the scenes from the *[Life of the Virgin](/wiki/Life_of_the_Virgin "Life of the Virgin")* or *Life of Christ* where he is present are far more often seen. The [Mérode Altarpiece](/wiki/M%C3%A9rode_Altarpiece "Mérode Altarpiece") of about 1425, where he has a panel to himself, working as a carpenter who fashions mousetraps – a rare depiction of Saint Joseph being present in the context of the [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation"). The fact that Saint Joseph makes mousetraps is attributed to an interpretation by [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo"), according to which Jesus Christ is the mousetrap for the devil.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

Some statues of Joseph depict his staff as topped with flowers, recalling the non-canonical Gospel of James's account of how Mary's spouse was chosen by collecting the walking sticks of widowers in Palestine, and Joseph's alone bursting into flower, thus identifying him as divinely chosen.[[141]](#cite_note-141) The *[Golden Legend](/wiki/Golden_Legend "Golden Legend")*, which derives its account from the much older [Gospel of Pseudo-Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Pseudo-Matthew "Gospel of Pseudo-Matthew"), tells a similar story, although it notes that all marriageable men of the Davidic line and not only widowers were ordered by the [High Priest](/wiki/Kohen_Gadol "Kohen Gadol") to present their rods at the [Temple](/wiki/Second_Temple "Second Temple"). Several [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") [Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus_in_art "Nativity of Jesus in art") [icons](/wiki/Icons "Icons") show Joseph tempted by the [Devil](/wiki/Devil "Devil") (depicted as an old man with furled wings) to break off his betrothal, and how he resists that temptation. There are some paintings with him wearing a [Jewish hat](/wiki/Jewish_hat "Jewish hat").[[142]](#cite_note-142)
